context_initialization.description: Starting point for each interaction (InkyPi plugin development)
steps: ALWAYS read `README.md` for project overview and supported hardware/plugin concepts
ALWAYS read `docs/building_plugins.md` before creating or modifying any plugin
ALWAYS read `docs/development.md` before changing anything outside a plugin folder
If the task touches rendering/layout, inspect the plugin output patterns used by existing plugins in `src/plugins/` before writing new code

operational_protocol.description: How to approach tasks for InkyPi plugins
before_action: Identify whether the change is: (A) new plugin, (B) plugin enhancement, or (C) shared/core change — prefer (A) or (B)
code_changes: Read relevant code sections before editing
Preserve existing comments and structure unless they are clearly wrong
Keep changes minimal and localized to the plugin folder whenever possible
Maintain backwards compatibility with existing plugin settings where feasible
development_responsibilities: You are responsible for developing InkyPi plugins inside `src/plugins/<your_plugin>/`
You may create or update plugin assets required by InkyPi (e.g., icon, settings template, plugin metadata) as described in `docs/building_plugins.md`
You are NOT responsible for refactoring the InkyPi core system unless explicitly requested
Do not change shared/core code to 'fit' a plugin; instead adapt the plugin to the existing plugin interface and patterns
plugin_building_rules: Place new plugins under `src/plugins/<plugin_slug>/` (plugin_slug should be lowercase and snake/kebab-safe)
Follow the plugin folder/file conventions described in `docs/building_plugins.md` (python class file, optional settings template, icon, and any required metadata files)
Match existing plugin patterns for: initialization, settings access, rendering output generation, and refresh/update behavior
Avoid doing heavy work at import time; defer network calls and expensive computations to the plugin execution path
All network calls MUST have explicit timeouts and graceful fallback output
logging_changes: If the project uses a changelog or notes file mentioned in `docs/development.md`, update it ONLY when explicitly asked (append-only; never rewrite history)
building_instructions: Never run install/update scripts or service restart commands automatically; provide the exact commands for the user to run
Do not assume hardware access (e-ink display) during development; keep plugins testable with mocked data where possible

safety_requirements: ALWAYS maintain proper error handling and user-friendly fallbacks (display something useful even on failure)
ALWAYS use explicit timeouts for HTTP/API calls
ALWAYS validate and sanitize user-provided settings (strings, URLs, file paths, units, numeric ranges)
NEVER log secrets (API keys/tokens) or embed them in plugin code; use the project’s settings/secret mechanism
NEVER add dependencies lightly; prefer stdlib. If a dependency is required, justify it and follow the project’s dependency workflow from `docs/development.md`
Respect e-ink constraints: minimize refresh frequency and avoid unnecessary redraws
coding_style: Use Python best practices and follow the existing style in the repository
Prefer type hints for public plugin methods and complex data structures
Use clear, descriptive names (snake_case for variables/functions, PascalCase for classes)
Keep functions small and focused; extract helpers for repeated logic
Add concise comments only where they explain non-obvious decisions (avoid redundant narration)
plugin_quality_standards: Plugins must degrade gracefully: missing configuration, API failure, or no network should not crash the plugin
Prefer deterministic rendering: the same settings should produce consistent output
Cache expensive results when appropriate (especially API calls) to reduce rate-limit risk and speed up refresh
Ensure settings have sensible defaults and safe bounds
code_organization: Keep all plugin-specific code, assets, and templates inside the plugin’s folder
Do not create catch-all utility modules; if shared helpers are truly needed, propose a minimal shared utility and keep it generic
Keep files reasonably sized (preferably under ~900 lines); split large files into focused modules
